one_line_takeaway: A broad dual-use survey of agentic AI as both cyber-defender and cyber-attacker that maps applications, attacks, and defenses onto lifecycle/NIST frameworks, backed by three deliberately minimal author-built Mistral/Ollama prototypes whose small synthetic-data wins (e.g., SOC triage recall 0.75->1.00, IDS AUROC 0.87->0.93) illustrate feasibility rather than prove impact.
key_finding: In the authors' own controlled synthetic prototypes, LLM-assisted agents modestly outperformed non-LLM baselines while adding human-readable rationales. SOC triage: the rule baseline had perfect precision but missed detections (recall 0.75, F1 0.86, MTTR 7.5 min); the Mistral agent reached perfect precision/recall/F1 (1.00/1.00/1.00) and cut simulated MTTR to 6.0 min, with all 12 agent actions policy-compliant and logged. Anomaly/IDS: baseline AUROC 0.87 / F1 0.74 vs. agent AUROC 0.93 / F1 0.81, with an explanation-usefulness rubric averaging 0.83. The survey's broader synthesis conclusion is that agentic AI works most reliably as augmentation, not replacement, with a persistent speed-vs-execution-authority tradeoff: low-risk actions are automated while irreversible/high-impact actions stay human-gated, because no surveyed system offers formal safety guarantees under distribution shift or adversarial manipulation.
core_contribution: A survey (36 pages) that treats agentic AI (LLM systems with planning loops, tool use, persistent memory, and multi-agent orchestration) as a first-class cybersecurity actor, arguing prior surveys were either model-centric (lifecycle safety) or domain-agnostic. The authors' OWN synthesis contributions are: (1) a taxonomy organizing agentic-AI security applications into four domains (Autonomous Cyber Defense and Operation, Agentic Threat Intelligence and Adversarial Analysis, Enterprise Security Automation and Governance, and Simulation/Training/Testing), each mapped to breach-lifecycle stages and NIST Cyber Defense functions (Table 3); (2) a taxonomy of agentic-AI-enabled ATTACKS (ransomware, insider threats, social engineering/fraud, exploitation/reconnaissance) (Table 4); (3) a consolidation of security-risk frameworks and defenses across perception/reasoning/action/memory layers (Tables 5-6); and (4) THREE original, deliberately minimal prototype implementations built by the authors (red-vs-blue adaptive agents, a SOC Tier-1 triage agent, and an anomaly/IDS agent), all using a lightweight local model (Mistral via Ollama) on synthetic data, intended to demonstrate feasibility rather than establish benchmarks. NOTE: much of the headline data in the paper (e.g., a 25-minute autonomous ransomware lifecycle, MTTE falling from 9 days to ~2 days, AI-in-cybersecurity spend of US$24.8B->US$146.5B) is CITED from Unit 42 [122] and industry reports [85], not the authors' own measurement.
threat_model: The survey does not propose a single threat model but synthesizes a dual-use one across the literature: agentic capabilities (planning, memory, tool orchestration, multi-agent interaction) simultaneously strengthen defense (continuous monitoring, autonomous incident response, threat hunting, fraud defense) and amplify offense (autonomous reconnaissance, adaptive exploitation, ransomware lifecycle compression, coordinated social engineering, oversight evasion). It emphasizes systemic risks not captured by traditional model-centric or lifecycle security frameworks: emergent agent collusion (including steganographic covert channels), cascading/irreversible failures, memory poisoning, prompt injection and unsafe tool invocation, session-management and model-pollution vulnerabilities, resource-abuse/cost-amplification DoS (e.g. CRABS-style), and governance/accountability gaps for long-lived autonomous agents acting under valid credentials.
limitations: The three prototypes are toy-scale and cannot support statistical claims: the SOC triage result rests on only SIX synthetic alerts, the IDS result on synthetic NetFlow windows, and the red/blue demo produces no quantitative metrics at all -- perfect 1.00 scores on six items are not evidence of real-world efficacy. All data is synthetic and the model is a single lightweight local LLM (Mistral), so results neither generalize nor probe adversarial robustness. As a survey it leans heavily on non-peer-reviewed vendor blogs, industry forecasts, and arXiv preprints, and it does not conduct systematic/PRISMA-style selection. Several headline figures are borrowed from cited industry sources rather than independently verified. There are also editorial defects (e.g., the OWASP/Microsoft framework paragraph in Section 6.1 is duplicated verbatim). The authors themselves frame the prototypes as feasibility illustrations, not benchmarks.

datasets_used: Synthetic system configuration file (open ports, service versions, known vulnerabilities incl. Apache 2.4.49 RCE) for the red/blue prototype -- author-generated

Six synthetic SOC alerts with ground-truth labels (Apache Struts RCE, SSH brute force, SQL injection, malware hash detection, suspicious user-agent, port scan) plus asset-criticality/threat-intel enrichment -- author-generated
Synthetic NetFlow-like time windows with features bytes/sec, pkts/sec, SYN rate, failed-connection rate, plus a small RAG note-set of benign vs malicious patterns -- author-generated
No real/named public benchmark datasets are used in the authors' own experiments
quantitative_results: SOC triage prototype (Table 8) - Baseline (rules): Precision 1.00, Recall 0.75, F1 0.86, Sim. MTTR 7.5 min
SOC triage prototype (Table 8) - Agent (Mistral): Precision 1.00, Recall 1.00, F1 1.00, Sim. MTTR 6.0 min; all 12 agent actions policy-compliant and logged; evaluated on 6 alerts
Anomaly/IDS prototype (Table 9) - Baseline (z-score + Isolation Forest): AUROC 0.87, F1 0.74
Anomaly/IDS prototype (Table 9) - Agent (Mistral): AUROC 0.93, F1 0.81; explanation-usefulness rubric average 0.83
Red/blue prototype: qualitative only, 2 adaptive rounds, no numeric metrics reported
CITED (not authors' own): Unit 42 [122] reports agents completing a full ransomware lifecycle in ~25 minutes and mean-time-to-exfiltrate dropping from 9 days (2021) to ~2 days (2024)
CITED (not authors' own): market projection of AI-in-cybersecurity spend US$24.8B (2024) -> US$146.5B (2034) [85]; He et al. [61] report >75% of malicious Bash commands execute without sandboxing
baselines_compared: SOC triage: deterministic rule-based/static-heuristic baseline vs. Mistral-via-Ollama LLM agent
Anomaly/IDS: z-score threshold + Isolation Forest baseline vs. Mistral-via-Ollama LLM agent with retrieval-augmented notes
Red/blue: no baseline (self-play red vs blue Mistral agents, qualitative)
